name: A curated list of awesome System Design (A.K.A. Distributed Systems) resources.
description: A curated list of awesome System Design (A.K.A. Distributed Systems) resources.

links.title: System Design Primer
links.description: [109k ⭐] - Awesome compilation of resources, including Anki flashcard decks.

links.title: System Design Interview Questions - Concepts you should know
links.description: A curated list of topics to introduce you to system design.

links.title: Grokking the System Design Interview
links.description: [Paid 💵] - Grokking System Design preparation is one of the most talked about course. The best thing about it is the design of applications it suggests rather than explanations of what each tool is supposed to do.

links.title: System Design in Software Development
links.description: Basic article on the topics of system design and architecture.

links.title: System Design
links.description: Introductory interview preparation resources.

links.title: Design Pattern for Distributed Systems
links.description: Article talking about some patterns as well as some technologies to be considered.

links.title: Practice system design problems using AI on Codemia.io
links.description: A tool which allows you to practice system design problems interactively like an interview with AI. There's iterative feedback and final evaluation which scores your performance

links.title: Distributed Computing
links.description: Wikipedia article broadening the view of distributed system design.

links.title: Fallacies of Distributed Computing
links.description: Wikipedia article introducing the topic of fallacies of distributed computing and its effects.

links.title: Fallacies of Distributed Computing Explained
links.description: In depth explanation of the fallacies mentioned above.

links.title: CAP Theorem
links.description: IBM Article about CAP Theorem, Microservices and NoSQL DBs.

links.title: Pattern: Microservice Architecture
links.description: Good article talking about Microservice architecture as well as its drawbacks.

links.title: Taxonomy of Distributed Systems
links.description: 11 Page lecture classifying distributed systems and specifically why we need them.

links.title: Top 10 Secure Coding Practices
links.description: Brief article talking about good practices for code securities.

links.title: Scalable Web Architecture and Distributed Systems
links.description: Good article about distributed systems as well as some of the potential tools.

links.title: Designing Distributed Systems: Patterns and Paradigms for Scalable, Reliable Services
links.description: [Paid 💵] - Book that talks about disitributed systems as well as lightly demonstrating some code of what it looks like.

links.title: Designing Data Intensive Applications
links.description: [Paid 💵] - Goes in depth to explain various resources we use when working with distributed systems, as well as how it came to be and what problems it aims to solve.

links.title: The System Design Manual
links.description: [Paid 💵] - Covers the core aspects of distributed systems, like: network fundamentals, the theory underpinning distributed systems, architectural patterns of scalable systems, stability patterns that harden systems against failures and operational best-practices on how to maintain large-scale systems with a small team.

links.title: Building Microservices
links.description: [Free 👍] - Awesome book that talks about designing sytem architecture with microservices in depth, includes most relevant topics in this regard.

links.title: Monolith to Microservices
links.description: [Free 👍] - Written by the same author as the one above, this book will cover the migration from Monolith to Microservices, it's recommended you start by the previous book.

links.title: Distributed Systems (3rd Edition)
links.description: [Free 👍] - Great overview of and in-depth introduction to distributed systems. Recommended for intermediate level readers.

links.title: Safe by Design
links.description: [Free 👍] - Great book on enforcing code correctness by design.

links.title: System Design Interview
links.description: YouTube channel focussed on content specific to system design interviews, with detailed explanation of a variety of problems.

links.title: Distributed Systems
links.description: This is an introductory course in Distributed Systems made by Chris Colohan. He got PhD from Carnegie Mellon, then spent 10 years working at Google building distributed systems.

links.title: The Easy Way
links.description: Up and coming channel with easy to understand videos about Distributed Systems.

links.title: MySQL
links.description: Widely used relational database.

links.title: SQLite
links.description: Another widely used database that is built into all mobile phones and most computers.

links.title: Sql Server
links.description: Widely used relational database.

links.title: Apache Ignite
links.description: [3.3k ⭐] - In memory caching with ACID properties.

links.title: Couchbase
links.description: Inspired by memcached, adding features such as replication and persistance.

links.title: Oracle Coherence
links.description: [126 ⭐] - High scaling, low latency in-memory caching.

links.title: Memcached
links.description: [10.2k ⭐] - One of the first in-memory caching database, high performing and multi-threaded.

links.title: Redis
links.description: [44k ⭐] - Widely used in-memory caching database with many added features such as persistent storage and supporting strings, lists, sets, hashses, streams, bitmaps, etc.

links.title: Apple FoundationDB
links.description: [10k ⭐] - Multi-model (many data types in a single database), ACID key-value store. Easily scalable and fault tolerant.

links.title: Cosmos DB
links.description: Microsoft's globally distributed, multi-model database service. Eastically and independently scale throughput and storage. SQL, MongoDB, Cassandra, Tables, Gremlin, and Spark APIs.

links.title: CouchDB
links.description: [4.6k ⭐] - ACID compliant NoSQL document-store DB, provides a RESTful HTTP API for reading and updating database documents.

links.title: MongoDB
links.description: One of the most popular 'NoSQL' database for general purpose.

links.title: ElasticSearch
links.description: [49.9k ⭐] - Widely popular 'NoSQL' database for fast and scalable search engines.

links.title: Amazon DynamoDB
links.description: Key-Value and Document database, highly performant, scalable and secure.

links.title: Google Bigtable
links.description: Scalable and performant 'NoSQL' database for large analytical and operational workload.

links.title: Cassandra
links.description: Facebook-born project very fast, easily scalable, with option to include consistency with each operation.

links.title: Scylla
links.description: [4.9k ⭐] - 'NoSQL' data store using seastar framework, compatible with Cassandra.

links.title: HBase
links.description: [3.6k ⭐] - Modeled after Google's Bigtable and written in Java. Developed as a part of Apache Hadoop project and runs on top of HDFS or Alluxio. (See [Hadoop Related](##hadoop-related))

links.title: Amazon Neptune
links.description: Fast, reliable and fully managed graph database service.

links.title: ArangoDB
links.description: [10k ⭐] - Flexible database for documents, key-value, graphs. Uses its own query language, AQL.

links.title: HDFS
links.description: Hadoop File System is a a widely popular choice among its big data competitors, providing high throughput access.

links.title: Lustre
links.description: File system for computer clusters.

links.title: CephFS
links.description: Unified, distributed storage system.

links.title: GlusterFS
links.description: Scale-out NAS file system.

links.title: MooseFS
links.description: POSIX-compliant distributed file system.

links.title: XtreemFS
links.description: Fault tolerant file system.

links.title: Kubernetes
links.description: Highly popular way to deploy, manage and automatically scale a cluster of containers on bare-metal or virtual servers.

links.title: Apache Samza
links.description: Build stateful applications that process data in real time from multiple sources, including Kafka. Easy and inexpensive multi-subscriber model, can eliminate backpressure and has reliable persistency with low latency.

links.title: Apache Flink
links.description: Based on the concept of streams and transofrmations. Uses maven, handles batch tasks as data streams with finite boundaries. Low latency, high throughput.

links.title: Amazon Kinesis Streams
links.description: Durable, scalable, real-tme service. Collects gigabytes of data per second from hundreds of thousands of sources, including database event streams, website clickstreams, financial transactions, etc.

links.title: Azure Stream Analytics
links.description: Real-time analytics service that is designed for mission-critical workloads.

links.title: Amazon MQ
links.description: Open source message broker from Amazon.

links.title: Apache ActiveMQ
links.description: It's a multi-protocol, java based messaging server.

links.title: Apache Kafka
links.description: Widely popular message broker with low latency for data streaming.

links.title: RabbitMQ
links.description: Widely popular lightweight

links.title: IronMQ
links.description: Very fast and highly scalable messaging broker. (not open source)

links.title: Apache Pulsar
links.description: Created by yahoo, also highly scalable, low latency, geo-replication and multi-tenacy.

links.title: Kestrel
links.description: Written in Scala and speaks the memcached protocol. It works much like Kafka.

links.title: Azure Service Bus
links.description: A fully managed enterprise integration message broker.

links.title: SeeSaw
links.description: [5.1k ⭐] - Used by Google, developed in Go, linux-based virtual load balancer server.

links.title: HAProxy
links.description: Widely popular option, provides high-availability, proxy, TCP/HTTP load balancing. Used by Reddit, Imgur, MaxCDN, GitHub, AirBNB.

links.title: Zevenet
links.description: Supports L3, L4 and L7. Easy install with a docker repo. Supports advanced health-check monitorining.

links.title: Neutrino
links.description: Used by eBay, built with Scala and Netty. Supports round-robin and least-connection algorithms.

links.title: Nginx
links.description: Wait, isn't Nginx a web server? Yes, the open source does support basic level of content switching and request routing. Plus edition supports load balancing, WAF, monitoring, etc.

links.title: Openresty
links.description: Nginx + Lua, perfect combination.

links.title: F5
links.description: Robust hardware load balancer option, supporting multiple protocols (IP, TCP, FTP, UDP, HTTP).

links.title: TP-Link
links.description: Cheaper alternative that works as a load balancer.

links.title: Barracuda
links.description: One of the top choices for load balancing when it comes to in-house servers. Top security measures built in, comprehensive reports and monitoring outbound traffic for data loss prevention.

links.title: Amazon Elastic Load Balancing
links.description: Popular choice for amazon customers, supports lambda functions, highly scalable.

links.title: Google Load Balancing
links.description: Popular choice for google customers, comes with auto-scaling feature, very fast, has intergrated CDN.

links.title: Cloudflare Load Balancing
links.description: Scalable load balancing by Cloudflare, feature fast failover and a dashboard.

links.title: DigitalOcean Load Balancing
links.description: If you're a digitalocean customer, this is a good option, very cheap, regional availability, scalable, easy to deploy among your other droplets.

links.title: Azure Load Balancing
links.description: Popular choice for Microsoft's Azure customers. Supports internal and external traffics, ipv6, monitorining and the standard load balancing set of features.

links.title: Ambari
links.description: Dashboard that integrates most of hadoop related technologies for easy management and executions.

links.title: Sqoop
links.description: Efficiently transfer data between Hadoop and structured datastores such as relational databases.

links.title: Flume
links.description: Distributed, highly available and efficient in collecting, aggregating and moving large amounts of log data.

links.title: Oozie
links.description: Create workflows in xml to execute jobs (from other hadoop-ecosystem applications) in steps, allows for parallel execution as well.

links.title: Hive
links.description: Query hadoop stored data in SQL.

links.title: Pig
links.description: Scriping language that looks like SQL to query hadoop data.

links.title: Tez
links.description: Solves a similar problem to Spark and MapReduce, it's more efficient than MapReduce because it calculates the most efficient way of doing it.

links.title: Map Reduce
links.description: MapReduce, as the name implies, maps data and reduce the results.

links.title: Spark
links.description: Powerful data processing to not only process data like Tez (and MapReduce), it can process streams of data in real time, apply regression analysis algorithms in ML and much more.

links.title: Apex
links.description: \*Retired project, it's a YARN-native platform that unifies stream and batch processing.

links.title: YARN
links.description: 'Yet Another Resource Negotiator', works like a kernel to manage computer resources across the clusters.

links.title: MESOS
links.description: Works like a Linux Kernel by managing CPU, memory, storage and other resources across the cluster.

links.title: Gin
links.description: [40.6k ⭐] - Blazingly fast microservice framework using Golang, high throughput capacity.

links.title: Phoenix
links.description: [15.5k ⭐] - Distributed processing, easily scalable, support for channels and live chat. This framework - written in Elixir, uses BEAM and Erlang, very efficient for large scale systems and supports high throughput.

links.title: Express.js
links.description: [49.6k ⭐] - Fast node.js rest api that can perform well under many scenarios.

links.title: Rails
links.description: [46.2k ⭐] - Written in Ruby, Rails delivers quick apis from prototype to production in an efficient manner.

links.title: Play Framework
links.description: [11.6k ⭐] - Very fast, high throughput framework written in Scala/Java that is RESTful by default.

links.title: Flask
links.description: [51.6k ⭐] - A lightweight Python Microframework for fast prototyping and production.

links.title: FastAPI
links.description: [22.7k ⭐] - A lightweight Python Microframework inspired in Flask but more modern, using Python async.

links.title: Django REST
links.description: [18.4k ⭐] - Written in Python, Django Rest is a powerful and flexible REST API. The efficiency and time to market resembles Rails.

links.title: ASP.NET Core MVC
links.description: A rich framework for building web apps and APIs using the Model-View-Controller design pattern in C# or F#. Number 6 on [TechEmpower Composite Benchmarks](https://www.techempower.com/benchmarks/#section=data-r19&hw=ph&test=composite) for web frameworks.

links.title: Fastify
links.description: [15.4k ⭐] - A Node.js web framework highly focused on providing the best developer experience with the least overhead and a powerful plugin architecture.
